Patch Set 4: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 4:

Looks good to me.

Patch Set 4: Code-Review+1

Instead of storing magic into one of the fields, could some kind of feature flag be set somewhere?

Patch Set 4:

> Instead of storing magic into one of the fields, could some kind of
 > feature flag be set somewhere?

Feature flags are for client-server feature negotiation, and the nodemap config is only accessed/used on the server, with local storage. 

It might be possible to do something like this (eg. store a nodemap config record that means "we are now storing zeroed records"), but even that is tricky because the nodemap config is stored in an index and not a list, so there is no inherent ordering of records.  Essentially, we would be burning a few bytes in the config record to indicate the rest of the bits are usable. The drawback is that we are burning a few bytes in the config record that we might need. 

Possibly this could be handled by a single bit in the primary nodemap config record (which we know is zeroed properly), that is only set if written for the first time (ie. we know all future records are zeroed). 

As an enhancement, if that "zeroed" bit is not set, then the code could read/zero/delete/write  every record in the nodemap config and then set the "zeroed" bit in the config.  We know that currently the records are (by definition) not using any of these unused fields, so it would be safe to zero them, and afterward they would be safe to use.

Patch Set 4:

> Patch Set 4:
> 
> > Instead of storing magic into one of the fields, could some kind of
>  > feature flag be set somewhere?
> 
> Feature flags are for client-server feature negotiation, and the nodemap config is only accessed/used on the server, with local storage. 
> 
> It might be possible to do something like this (eg. store a nodemap config record that means "we are now storing zeroed records"), but even that is tricky because the nodemap config is stored in an index and not a list, so there is no inherent ordering of records.  Essentially, we would be burning a few bytes in the config record to indicate the rest of the bits are usable. The drawback is that we are burning a few bytes in the config record that we might need. 
> 
> Possibly this could be handled by a single bit in the primary nodemap config record (which we know is zeroed properly), that is only set if written for the first time (ie. we know all future records are zeroed). 
> 
> As an enhancement, if that "zeroed" bit is not set, then the code could read/zero/delete/write  every record in the nodemap config and then set the "zeroed" bit in the config.  We know that currently the records are (by definition) not using any of these unused fields, so it would be safe to zero them, and afterward they would be safe to use.

I'm trying to think if there's some kind of preexisting set of flags for nodemap or disk features stored on disk. Based on your reply, it seems we'd have to add a new bit field to this primary config record. Rather than having a bit just for "zero'd or not zero'd", it seems more straightforward to have a bit for each new feature that requires a record field?

parents.subject: LU-17906 pltrpc: don't use non-uptodate peer at connect

commit.subject: LU-18247 nodemap: initialize unused fields on disk
commit.message:
LU-18247 nodemap: initialize unused fields on disk

Initialize the unused fields in the on-disk nodemap records to zero.
This will allow them to be used in the future for expanded nodemap
functionality.

It may be that initial usage will also depend on storing a magic
into one of the fields so that the rest of the fields can be trusted.
